elements.sent-2411-001: We present the results of our system for the CoMeDi Shared Task, which predicts majority votes (Subtask 1) and annotator disagreements (Subtask 2).
elements.sent-2411-002: Our approach combines model ensemble strategies with MLP-based and threshold-based methods trained on pretrained language models.
elements.sent-2411-003: Treating individual models as virtual annotators, we simulate the annotation process by designing aggregation measures that incorporate continuous similarity scores and discrete classification labels to capture both majority and disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we employ anisotropy removal techniques to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods, particularly for Subtask 2.
elements.sent-2411-006: Notably, we find that continuous similarity scores, even within the same model, align better with human disagreement patterns compared to aggregated discrete labels.
elements.sent-2411-007: Lexical semantic similarity is a classical task that encompasses various forms, including multi-choice sense selection~\cite{2009wsd , binary classification~\cite{pilehvar2019wic , and contextual word similarity~\cite{islam2008similarity , among others.}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the potential disagreements among annotators, arising from the inherent vagueness and continuous nature of meaning, have received comparatively less attention.
elements.sent-2411-009: To address these complexities, the CoMedi workshop (Context and Meaning - Navigating Disagreements in NLP Annotations\footnote{\url{https://comedinlp.github.io/ ) introduced a Shared Task with two subtasks~\cite{schlechtweg2025comedi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The first subtask involves predicting the median judgment classification across four candidate labels, which represent the degree of similarity for a target word in context.
elements.sent-2411-011: The second subtask focuses on predicting annotator disagreement, which can be interpreted as uncertainty estimation~\cite{gal2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we first conceptualize the two subtasks as corresponding to two fundamental statistical properties of a Gaussian distribution: the mean and variance.
elements.sent-2411-013: Subsequently, we model each system, parameterized by specific variables, as an individual human annotator.
elements.sent-2411-014: These variables encompass both homogeneous factors, such as layers within the same model, and heterogeneous factors across different models.
elements.sent-2411-015: To address the tasks, we employ MLP-based and threshold-based approaches to generate continuous similarity scores and discrete classification labels, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-016: Additionally, we incorporate techniques for anisotropy removal to mitigate geometric biases inherent in embedding spaces.
elements.sent-2411-017: Finally, we propose diverse strategies for model ensembling to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-018: Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of threshold-based methods combined with anisotropy removal and MLP-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-019: For Subtask 2, the findings further highlight the advantages of aggregating similarity scores over discrete labels in capturing annotator disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-020: Tasks capturing word meaning in context include word sense disambiguation (WSD)~\cite{2009wsd , which selects the most appropriate sense, and WiC~\cite{pilehvar2019wic , which determines semantic equivalence across contexts.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Extending these, similarity scoring provides a continuous measure of semantic relatedness.
elements.sent-2411-022: The CoMeDi Shared Task reframes WiC as a multiple-choice task with four labels indicating similarity degrees.
elements.sent-2411-023: Probing methods include MLP-based approaches, which train dense networks, and threshold-based methods, which optimize similarity thresholds for pretrained representations~\cite{vulic2020probing .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Since embeddings are often anisotropic~\cite{ethayarajh2019how , techniques like centering~\cite{centering and standardization~\cite{standardization are applied to improve representation quality.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Subtask 2 models annotator disagreement, aligning with the study of uncertainty estimation (UE), widely explored in computer vision~\cite{gal2017deep and robust AI~\cite{stutz2022understanding .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: UE arises from data uncertainty (aleatoric, linked to inherent data ambiguity like annotation disagreement) and model uncertainty (epistemic, due to biased learning on out-of-distribution data)~\cite{gal2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Researchers~\cite{liu2023ambiguity combine these areas to model semantic uncertainty in sense selection.}
elements.sent-2411-028: While Bayesian~\cite{vazhentsev2022uncertainty and non-Bayesian~\cite{LabelSmooth methods often use label probabilities, our threshold-based method lacks this feature.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Instead, we treat the process as model ensemble~\cite{deepensemble and propose aggregation measures.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Most systems use MLP-based and threshold-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-031: They extract representations from pretrained language models, then MLP-based methods train a network to predict discrete labels (Subtask 1) or continuous values (Subtask 2).
elements.sent-2411-032: Threshold-based methods learn a threshold to map similarity scores to labels.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, naive baselines often fall short, as shown in Section~\ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In our system, we unify both subtasks and modify threshold-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-035: For Subtask 1, we apply techniques to make data points more isotropic.
elements.sent-2411-036: For Subtask 2, we ensemble models, treating them as annotators, and use various strategies to model disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-037: For a target word $w$ appearing in a pair of context $c_i$ and $c_j$, hypothetically infinitive annotators from a ``human'' space $\mathcal{H}$ provide a judgment score $s \in \mathcal{R}$, where a higher value indicates a more similar meaning between $c_i$ and $c_j$.
elements.sent-2411-038: These scores consist of a judgment distribution $p$ on $\mathcal{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-039: We hypothesize that the distribution converges a Gaussian distribution, i.e., $p \sim \mathcal{N (\mu, \sigma^2)$, as it is the most natural choice in statistics~\cite{jaynes2003probability .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In this distribution, $\mu$ reflects an averaged similarity degree among people, or a consensus.
elements.sent-2411-041: On the other side, $\sigma$ shows the disagreement among each other.
elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore, Subtask 1 and Subtask 2 are designed to estimate $\mu$ and $\sigma$ in this perspective.
elements.sent-2411-043: Contextual representations are known to be anisotropic~\cite{ethayarajh2019how , geometrically gathering into one corner, and thus undermining the comparison of cosine similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-044: We adopt three methods across the samples to alleviate this issue.
elements.sent-2411-045: To model annotator disagreement, we treat each model or its manipulation as an annotator and use three measures to reflect uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-046: We explore three ensembling strategies: (1) homogeneous aggregation with model manipulations (e.g., layer and anisotropy removal), (2) heterogeneous ensembling across different models, and (3) a mixed approach combining both.
elements.sent-2411-047: After each model forward pass, we obtain a discrete label using the threshold-based model~\footnote{For Subtask 2, we use the majority of judgment scores as the GT label, avoiding the median to handle decimals.}
elements.sent-2411-048: We apply three measures: standard deviation (STD) for continuous scores, mean pairwise absolute judgment differences (MPD) for discrete labels (as used in Subtask 2), and variation ratio (VR), the ratio of values not equal to the mode, commonly used in uncertainty estimation~\cite{gal2017deep .}

elements.sent-2411-051: The task data was sampled from multilingual datasets, involving 7 languages, i.e., Chinese~\cite{Chen2023chiwug , English~\cite{Schlechtweg2024dwugs , German~\cite{Schlechtweg2024dwugs , Norwegian~\cite{kutuzov2022nordiachange , Russian~\cite{rodina2020rusemshift, rushifteval2021 , Spanish~\cite{Zamora2022lscd , Swedish~\cite{Schlechtweg2024dwugs .}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-053: The first three are encoder-only models, where we extract representations from the target word position.
elements.sent-2411-054: For the decoder-only Llama-7B, we use a prompt (see Appendix~\ref{app:prompt ) as in~\cite{liu2023ambiguity and extract the final colon representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: We apply layer-wise manipulations (centering, standardization, and all-but-the-top) to reduce anisotropy.
elements.sent-2411-056: The optimal threshold is found using the training set, and the top four models are selected based on development set performance.
elements.sent-2411-057: For Subtask 2, we ensemble models with different strategies (homo, hete, mixed) and measures (STD, MPD, VR).
elements.sent-2411-058: We also test the MLP-based model, as detailed in Appendix~\ref{app:setting .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Tables~\ref{tab:sub1 and~\ref{tab:sub2 show the evaluation phase scores of the top three teams for Subtask 1 and Subtask 2, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The evaluation phase consists of the leaderboard submission with hidden answers, while in the post-evaluation phase, the test labels are released by the organizers.
elements.sent-2411-061: Our post-evaluation results are competitive, particularly for Subtask 2.
elements.sent-2411-062: The optimal ensembling strategies are standardization with layers 8 and 16, none with layer 8, and centering with layer 16.
elements.sent-2411-063: Figure~\ref{fig:stds shows the average performance change with different anisotropy removal methods across layers.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The large gap between removal and non-removal emphasizes the importance of this technique.
elements.sent-2411-065: Performance improves with higher layers, except for a drop in the last one or two layers.
elements.sent-2411-066: Standardization consistently performs best across all layers.
elements.sent-2411-067: Figure~\ref{fig:models displays the performance of different models.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Since Llama-7B is a decoder-only model with significantly more parameters and training data, its optimal result (Layer 25) serves as an upper bound.

elements.sent-2411-070: In this section, we analyze various factors influencing ensembling performance, including the choice of measure and model selection.
elements.sent-2411-071: We evaluate four candidate models with four types of anisotropy removal and four layer levels.
elements.sent-2411-072: For layer levels, we extract layers 1, 4, 7, 10 for encoder-only models, and layers 8, 16, 24, 32 for the Llama model, yielding 64 possible model configurations.
elements.sent-2411-073: We randomly select a subset of 4 models from these possibilities, referred to as "mixed".
elements.sent-2411-074: Additionally, we experiment with homogeneous aggregation (using the same model) and heterogeneous aggregation (using different models).
elements.sent-2411-075: For homogeneous aggregation, we choose Llama-7B due to its superior performance.
elements.sent-2411-076: For each category, we sample 500 model subsets, obtaining both their classification labels using a threshold-based method and similarity scores based on pre-trained embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-077: We first evaluate three measures (STD, MPD, and VR) in the mixed setting, selecting the best one to compare different category choices.
elements.sent-2411-078: Figure~\ref{fig:measure presents the results for three measures.}
elements.sent-2411-079: In most cases, STD outperforms the others, while MDP slightly exceeds VR.
elements.sent-2411-080: This suggests that similarity scores have an advantage over discrete labels due to the robustness of continuous values.
elements.sent-2411-081: Label prediction can be seen as a discretization of the continuous counterpart, leading to a loss of precision.
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus, we select STD as our final measure.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/measure.pdf} \caption{Performance of three types of measures across 500 random runs.} \label{fig:measure}
elements.sent-2411-083: Table~\ref{tab:ensembles_type shows the top five results for three ensemble strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The specific model groups are listed in Appendix~\ref{app:models .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Homogeneous model manipulations (homo) outperform mixed ensembles, while combining different models yields the worst performance.
elements.sent-2411-086: This suggests that model variance can still serve as an effective alternative, aligning with the use of dropout in uncertainty estimation~\cite{gal2017deep .}

elements.sent-2411-087: We present our system for two subtasks released on CoMeDi Shared Task.
elements.sent-2411-088: We first formalize these tasks as parameter estimation where Subtask 1 estimates a mean and Subtask 2 the variance for a hypothetical Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-089: Then we mainly adopt threshold-based method with different techniques of anisotropy removal to classify the label for Subtask 1.
elements.sent-2411-090: Inspired by the area of uncertainty estimation, we utilize model ensembling with various strategies to select models and measures to reflect disagreement for Subtask 2.
elements.sent-2411-091: Experiments show the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-092: Further work could include discovering the reason behind the different degrees of disagreement and exploring the potential of large language models.

elements.sent-2411-093: The prompt is The $w$ in this sentence: $c$ means in one word :.
elements.sent-2411-094: We extract the representation of the ending colon.
elements.sent-2411-095: We use the xlm-roberta-base model to extract text vectors and apply mean pooling on the output of the last four layers.
elements.sent-2411-096: A neural network with two linear layers is trained to reduce dimensionality and map the vectors to classification labels, using the ReLU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-097: Training settings for the MLP-based method on Subtask 1: 50 epochs, batch size of 128, learning rate of 1e-2, AdamW optimizer, and a dropout rate of 0.1 to improve generalization.

elements.sent-2411-099: X, Y, Z, and W correspond to four standardization methods: non-standard, std, centering, and all-but-the-top.
elements.sent-2411-100: Treating individual models as virtual annotators, we simulate the annotation process by designing aggregation measures that incorporate continuous relatedness scores and discrete classification labels to capture both majority and disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-101: The code will be published at \url{https://github.com/RyanLiut/CoMeDi_Solution .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Subtask 1 involves predicting the median judgment classification across four candidate labels, which represent the degree of similarity for a target word in context.
elements.sent-2411-103: Subtask 2 focuses on predicting annotator disagreement, which can be interpreted as a form of predictive uncertainty estimation~\cite{gal2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-104: To address the tasks, we employ MLP-based and threshold-based approaches to generate continuous relatedness~\footnote{\textcolor{black {We distinguish similarity from relatedness, with the task focusing on annotating relatedness scores.}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: For Subtask 2, the findings further highlight the advantages of aggregating relatedness scores over discrete labels in capturing annotator disagreement.
elements.sent-2411-106: Extending these, relatedness scoring provides a continuous measure of semantic relatedness.
elements.sent-2411-107: The CoMeDi Shared Task reframes WiC as an ordinal classification task with four labels indicating relatedness degrees.
elements.sent-2411-108: Probing methods include MLP-based approaches~\cite{tenney2019you,pilehvar2019wic , which train dense networks, and threshold-based methods~\cite{pilehvar2019wic,vulic2020probing,liu-etal-2024-fantastic , which optimize relatedness thresholds for pretrained representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Annotator disagreement is common in lexical semantics tasks, such as word sense disambiguation (WSD)~\cite{2009wsd,chklovski2003exploiting , due to the subjective and ambiguous nature of meaning~\cite{navigli2008structural .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: While many studies resolve disagreement through majority voting, others exploit it by reframing tasks as multi-label classification~\cite{conia2021framing or training on multiple judgments~\cite{uma2021learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: In this paper, we model annotator disagreement as uncertainty estimation, as both involve (1) output variability, (2) data noise~\footnote{Annotator disagreement can be viewed as label noise, contributing to data uncertainty—a key component of irreducible uncertainty.}
elements.sent-2411-113: Threshold-based methods learn a threshold selector to map similarity scores to labels.
elements.sent-2411-114: In our system, we applied anisotropy removal to the baseline code~\cite{schlechtweg2025comedi and used a classifier-based method for comparison.}
elements.sent-2411-115: For a target word \(w\) appearing in a pair of contexts \(c_i\) and \(c_j\), annotators from a hypothetical human space \(\mathcal{H}\) provide a judgment score \(s \in \mathcal{R}\), where higher values indicate greater similarity in meaning between \(c_i\) and \(c_j\).
elements.sent-2411-116: These scores form a judgment distribution \(p\) on \(\mathcal{R}\), which we assume follows a Gaussian distribution, \(p \sim \mathcal{N (\mu, \sigma^2)\), as it is a natural statistical choice~\cite{jaynes2003probability .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Here, \(\mu\) represents the consensus similarity, while \(\sigma\) reflects disagreement among annotators.
elements.sent-2411-118: In practice, the continuous Gaussian distribution is discretized due to the finite number of annotators and graded annotations.
elements.sent-2411-119: Nonetheless, we adopt the Gaussian framework to unify the two tasks: Subtask 1 estimates \(\mu\), while Subtask 2 estimates \(\sigma\).
elements.sent-2411-120: This inflates similarity scores, reducing their discriminative power in meaning-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, even unrelated words often exhibit high similarity.
elements.sent-2411-122: We adopt three techniques to reduce anisotropy: (1) centering by subtracting the mean vector (2) normal standardization (3) All-but-the-top~\cite{all-but-the-top : subtracting the projection on the component of the largest variance.}

elements.sent-2411-125: Our study focuses on threshold-based methods using pre-trained models: XLM-RoBERTa-base, XLM-RoBERTa-large~\cite{conneau2019unsupervised , BERT-multi-base~\cite{pires2019multilingual , and Llama-7B~\cite{touvron2023llama .}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: For encoder-only models, we extract target word representations directly, while for the decoder-only Llama-7B, we use a prompt-based method~\cite{liu2023ambiguity to extract the final colon representation.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Inspired by in-context learning~\cite{jiang-etal-2024-scaling , we apply layer-wise manipulations (centering, standardization, and all-but-the-top) to reduce anisotropy.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We discretize the continuous similarity scores into labels using a threshold selector based on the shared task baseline.
elements.sent-2411-129: The selector employs the Nelder-Mead method~\cite{nelder1965simplex to optimize bin edges for Krippendorff's $\alpha$, starting with evenly spaced bins and iteratively refining them.}
elements.sent-2411-130: For Subtask 2, we explore model ensembling strategies (homo, hetero, mixed) and different measures (STD, MPD, VR), and also evaluate an MLP-based approach (details in Appendix~\ref{app:setting ).}
elements.sent-2411-131: During the evaluation phase, we selected models based on the development set.
elements.sent-2411-132: For Subtask 1, we employ a threshold-based method using XLM-RoBERTa-base as the pretrained model, except for Chinese and Russian (BERT-multi-base) and Norwegian (LERT-base-chinese).
elements.sent-2411-133: Representations are extracted from the 10th layer for XLM-RoBERTa-base and the final layer for other models.
elements.sent-2411-134: We apply normal standardization except for Norwegian to address anisotropy and utilized the threshold selection method from the official baseline code~\cite{schlechtweg2025comedi .}
elements.sent-2411-135: Specifically, For Subtask 2, we fine-tune an MLP regressor to predict disagreement scores, following the baseline methodology.
elements.sent-2411-136: It comprised of two linear layers and a ReLU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-137: For Swiss, we train for 50 epochs with a batch size of 32, while for other languages, we use 200 epochs with a batch size of 16.
elements.sent-2411-138: The learning rate is 1e-2 with a 0.1 dropout rate.
elements.sent-2411-139: We utilize AdamW for optimization with a warm-up ratio of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-140: For Subtask 1, we use the 25th layer of Llama and the 11th layer of XLM-RoBERTa-Base for all languages, with an MLP-based method fine-tuned using training data.
elements.sent-2411-141: All model representations are standardized to remove anisotropy.
elements.sent-2411-142: For the MLP-based model, we train for 50 epochs with a batch size of 128, an initial learning rate of 1e-2, and apply a dropout rate of 0.1 to prevent overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-143: For Subtask 2, we employ ensembling strategies to significantly improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-144: We report two results from our ensembling methods.
elements.sent-2411-145: The first (ensembling) applies the same strategy across all languages: standardization with layer 24, no standardization with layer 16, centering with layer 24, and all-but-the-top with layer 16, all on Llama-7B.
elements.sent-2411-146: The second (ensembling*) presents language-specific ensembling strategies, as in Table~\ref{tab:specific_lanuage .}
elements.sent-2411-147: We present the results in Table~\ref{tab:sub1 and Table~\ref{tab:sub2 on the test set.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: The upper sections show evaluation phase scores submitted to the leaderboard, while the lower sections display post-evaluation results using public answers.
elements.sent-2411-149: We then conduct abalation studies on the development set in later sections.
elements.sent-2411-150: In the evaluation phrase, for Subtask 1, our threshold-based method achieved moderate results, with LERT-base-chinese performing relatively better for Norwegian, though with limitations.
elements.sent-2411-151: For Subtask 2, we fine-tuned an MLP to predict disagreement scores but observed limited performance, prompting alternative methods in the post-evaluation phase.
elements.sent-2411-152: In the post-evaluation phase, for Subtask 1, the threshold-based model performed comparably to the MLP-based model, while large language models (LLMs) showed superior results, highlighting their potential.
elements.sent-2411-153: For Subtask 2, our results matched the evaluation phase’s top performances, confirming the effectiveness of the ensembling approach.
elements.sent-2411-154: Since Llama-7B is a decoder-only model with significantly more parameters and training data, its optimal result (Layer 25) serves as an upper bound~\footnote{We attempt representations of different layers from Llama-7B, and the optimal layer index is 25.. .}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/xlm-base_stds_dev.pdf} \caption{Performance of different types of anisotropy removal with the increase of layer index. 0 indicates the input embedding. ``abtt'' means all-but-the-top.} \label{fig:stds}
elements.sent-2411-155: We evaluate four candidate models \ie, XLM-RoBERTa-base, XLM-RoBERTa-large, BERT-multi-base, and Llama-7B, with four types of anisotropy removal and four layer levels.
elements.sent-2411-156: We use a threshold-based method for each model to obtain both a continuous similarity score and a discrete classification label, as we have done in Subtask 1.
elements.sent-2411-157: For each category, we sample 500 model subsets, obtaining both their classification labels using a threshold-based method and relatedness scores based on pre-trained embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-158: In most cases, STD on a continuous similarity score outperforms the others, while MDP slightly exceeds VR on the discrete classification labels .
elements.sent-2411-159: The specific model groups are listed in Tabel~\ref{tab:ensembles_type_names .}
elements.sent-2411-160: We acknowledge several limitations in our system.
elements.sent-2411-161: First, the model training process utilizes data from all languages without considering their unique linguistic characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-162: For instance, Chinese exhibits rich formation rules~\cite{zheng-etal-2021-leveraging-word , yet lacks the morphological complexity found in Western languages, potentially leading to distinct patterns of disagreement.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Second, our parameter estimation for the Gaussian distribution does not account for the estimation of the mean, which could be incorporated into Subtask 1 for a more comprehensive approach.
elements.sent-2411-164: Furthermore, in Subtask 2, we employ the median of all annotations as an independent label for the model instead of using individual annotations.
elements.sent-2411-165: This approach may introduce inconsistencies with our formulation of models as annotators.
elements.sent-2411-166: Lastly, while our experiments highlight the potential of large language models (LLMs) compared to pretrained language models, future work will focus on exploring more effective strategies for extracting lexical representations from LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-167: We do not foresee any immediate negative ethical consequences arising from our research.
elements.sent-2411-168: The authors thank the anonymous reviewers for their valuable comments and constructive feedback on the manuscript.
elements.sent-2411-169: This work is supported by the 2018 National Major Program of Philosophy and Social Science Fund “Analyses and Researches of Classic Texts of Classical Literature Based on Big Data Technology” (18ZDA238) and Research on the Long-Term Goals and Development Plan for National Language and Script Work by 2035 (ZDA145-6).

elements.sent-2411-170: We attempt the MLP-based method in two subtasks, freezing XLM-RoBERTa-base model parameters to obtain the vector representation of the target word, and training a classifier or a regression model downstream.
elements.sent-2411-171: MLP1 is a linear layer, MLP2 represents two linear layers, and uses the ReLU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-172: In Subtask 1, we use the cross-entropy loss function to train a classifier.
elements.sent-2411-173: The results on the development dataset are shown in Table~\ref{tab:sub4 .}
elements.sent-2411-174: We find that two linear layers achieve better results.
elements.sent-2411-175: We attempt to use a weighted cross-entropy loss function to alleviate the problem of sample imbalance, but shows slight improvement.
elements.sent-2411-176: We compare the results of different layers of the model and find that the vector representation of the shallower layers(11th) achieves better results.
elements.sent-2411-177: We attempt layer fusion and average pooling of the vectors in the last 4 layers, which results in more stable improvements.
elements.sent-2411-178: Training settings for the MLP-based method in Subtask 1: 50 epochs, batch size of 128, 1e-2 learning rate, AdamW optimizer, and a dropout rate of 0.1 to improve generalization.
elements.sent-2411-179: In Subtask 2, we use the mean square error loss function to train a regression model that directly predicts continuous values of inconsistent labeling of target words, similar to the baseline provided by the official source.
elements.sent-2411-180: The results on the development dataset are shown in Table~\ref{tab:sub5 .}
elements.sent-2411-181: We find that two linear layers are worse than a single linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-182: We try multiple different hyperparameter settings on Task 2.
elements.sent-2411-183: In MLP1, we ultimately chose 200 epochs, batch size of 16, while in MLP2, we chose 50 epochs, batch size of 32.
elements.sent-2411-184: Other training settings: 1e-2 learning rate, AdamW optimizer, and a dropout rate of 0.1.
elements.sent-2411-185: We use letters to denote different models: A, B, C, and D represent Llama-7B, XLM-RoBERTa-base, BERT-multi-base, and XLM-RoBERTa-large, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-186: For encoder-only models, h, i, j, and k indicate layers 1, 4, 7, and 10, respectively; whereas in large language models (LLMs), these symbols correspond to layers 8, 16, 24, and 32.
elements.sent-2411-187: We experiment with various model groups, and different groups achieve the best results in different languages.
elements.sent-2411-188: Table~\ref{tab:sub2 shows the best results for the test dataset in Subtask 2, and the specific model groups are shown in Table~\ref{tab:specific_lanuage .}}
elements.sent-2411-189: We employ three ensemble strategies, and the top five results of each strategy on the development dataset of Subtask 2 are presented in Table 3, with corresponding model groups shown in Table~\ref{tab:ensembles_type_names .}
